documents.title: Seed Saving and Storage
documents.content: Open-pollinated seeds are best for long-term seed saving because they reproduce true to type when isolated properly. Harvest mature seeds from healthy plants, clean and dry them thoroughly, then store in cool, dark, low-humidity conditions with clear labels for crop, variety, and year.

documents.title: Composting Basics
documents.content: Maintain a balanced compost mix of carbon-rich material (dry leaves, straw, paper) and nitrogen-rich material (fresh plant scraps, manure from healthy animals). Keep moisture around a wrung-out sponge level and turn for airflow. If compost smells rotten, add dry carbon and aerate.

documents.title: Human Waste Compost Safety
documents.content: Human waste can carry dangerous pathogens and must be managed with strict separation, controlled compost process, and long maturation before any reuse. Follow proven public-health guidance for your climate and system design. If safe processing cannot be guaranteed, do not use human-waste compost on food crops.

documents.title: Crop Rotation and Companion Planting
documents.content: Rotate crop families across beds each season to reduce pests and nutrient depletion. Use companion planting for support and ground cover, but verify local conditions rather than relying on universal formulas. Record outcomes each season so planting plans improve over time.

documents.title: Root Cellars and Cool Storage
documents.content: Cool, stable, humid storage extends shelf life without electricity. Separate produce types that release ethylene (like apples) from crops sensitive to it. Inspect storage weekly and remove spoiled produce quickly to protect the rest.

documents.title: Small Livestock Planning
documents.content: Choose animals based on available feed, shelter, climate, and labor. Build routines for water, disease observation, breeding control, and manure handling. Diversify food sources and avoid dependence on a single animal type.

documents.title: Wild Foraging Safety
documents.content: Never consume an unidentified plant or mushroom. Use multiple identification traits and, if possible, a second knowledgeable confirmation. Learn and prioritize a short list of high-confidence local species before expanding your foraging range.
